title: Introduction to Leadership
content:
Module 2, Lesson 1: Introduction to Leadership

The Hook

What separates a manager from a leader? Management is the discipline of making things work; leadership is the discipline of making people want to make things work. Warren Bennis captured the distinction memorably: "Managers do things right; leaders do the right things." Yet in practice, the best people in organisations must do both — exercising management when precision and control are needed, and leadership when direction, inspiration, and change are required.

Leadership is one of the most studied topics in the social sciences. Thousands of studies, hundreds of frameworks, and decades of executive development programmes have sought to answer one fundamental question: what makes an effective leader? This lesson provides the conceptual foundation for Module 2's exploration of that question.

The Concept

1. Defining Leadership

Leadership can be defined as the process of influencing others to achieve a common goal. Three elements are essential: influence (leadership operates through persuasion and inspiration, not just authority); others (leadership is inherently relational — it requires followers); and common goal (leadership is purposeful, oriented toward collective outcomes). This definition distinguishes leadership from coercion (which compels through threat), management (which coordinates through structure), and expertise (which influences through knowledge alone). A leader's power may be positional (derived from role authority) or personal (derived from expertise, character, and relationships).

2. Leadership vs. Management

John Kotter's framework distinguishes the two functions clearly. Management involves: planning and budgeting, organising and staffing, controlling and problem-solving — producing predictability and order. Leadership involves: setting direction (vision), aligning people (communication and coalition-building), and motivating and inspiring (energising people to overcome obstacles to change). Both are necessary. Organisations without good management produce chaos; organisations without good leadership produce stagnation. The challenge for most people in leadership roles is that management skills are often more easily taught, measured, and rewarded — creating a systematic underinvestment in leadership development.

3. Sources of Leadership Power

French and Raven (1959) identified five bases of social power. Legitimate power derives from formal position. Reward power derives from ability to provide incentives. Coercive power derives from ability to punish. Expert power derives from knowledge and skill. Referent power derives from personal admiration and respect. Research consistently shows that expert and referent power produce the highest levels of follower motivation, creativity, and commitment. Legitimate, reward, and coercive power, while necessary, tend to produce compliance rather than commitment — and can backfire when followers question their legitimacy.

4. The Evolution of Leadership Theory

Leadership theory has evolved through several generations. Trait theory (1900s–1940s) sought innate leadership characteristics. Behavioural theory (1940s–1960s) examined what leaders do. Situational/contingency theory (1960s–1980s) recognised that effective leadership depends on context. Transformational theory (1980s–present) focused on inspiring followers to transcend self-interest. Authentic leadership theory emphasises leading from values and self-awareness. Each generation added complexity and nuance, moving from the simplistic "great man" theory to the sophisticated, multi-dimensional models used today.

5. The Leader-Follower Relationship

Modern leadership theory increasingly recognises that leadership is co-created in the relationship between leader and follower. Leader-Member Exchange (LMX) theory demonstrates that leaders develop differentiated relationships with different team members: high-quality LMX relationships (characterised by trust, respect, and mutual obligation) produce significantly better performance, commitment, and career outcomes than low-quality relationships. This insight has practical implications: effective leaders deliberately invest in building high-quality relationships across their entire team, not just with a favoured inner circle.

The Illustration

Leadership is a discipline that can be studied, practised, and developed. Understanding its definition, its relationship to management, the sources of leader power, the evolution of theory, and the dynamics of leader-follower relationships provides the conceptual foundation for the detailed models explored in subsequent lessons. The best leaders continuously develop their self-awareness, adapt their style, and invest in the relationships through which all leadership ultimately operates.

quiz.question: How did John Kotter distinguish leadership from management?
options: Leadership produces order; management produces change
Management involves planning and controlling for predictability; leadership sets direction and motivates for change
Leadership is only relevant at senior levels

quiz.question: According to French and Raven, which source of power produces the highest follower commitment?
What does Leader-Member Exchange (LMX) theory focus on?
options: The differentiated quality of relationships between leaders and individual team members
How leaders adapt style to follower maturity
The impact of leadership on organisational strategy

quiz.question: Which of the following best defines leadership?
options: The process of controlling people through authority
The process of influencing others to achieve a common goal
The administration of rules and procedures

quiz.question: Which generation of leadership theory first recognised that effective leadership depends on the situation?
